nl_assertions: Must explicitly explain the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly define it (difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening with daytime impairment).
Must assess key insomnia history: onset/duration, frequency, sleep schedule, sleep latency, awakenings, total sleep time, daytime consequences, and precipitating/maintaining factors.

Must screen for secondary causes and red flags: depression/anxiety/mania, suicidality, substance/alcohol/caffeine/nicotine use, medication contributors (stimulants, steroids, decongestants), pain, thyroid symptoms, and pregnancy when relevant.
Must screen for other sleep disorders when indicated: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.
Must recommend CBT-I as first-line treatment and describe at least two components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Must explicitly state to avoid butobarbital (and generally avoid barbiturates) due to high risk (dependence, overdose/respiratory depression, interactions).

Before any prescribing, must perform safety checks in conversation: allergy check, contraindication check (e.g., pregnancy, severe hepatic disease, untreated OSA/respiratory compromise if sedatives considered, history of substance use disorder), and drug interaction check (especially CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines).
If offering medication, must use shared decision-making, discuss risks/benefits, and counsel to avoid alcohol/other sedatives and to use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
Must include a follow-up timeframe (e.g., 2-4 weeks) and clear return precautions (worsening mood/suicidality, severe daytime somnolence, suspected sleep apnea symptoms, medication adverse effects).
Communication should be clear, nonjudgmental, and should set expectations that CBT-I typically provides the most durable benefit.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, prioritize CBT-I, use a low-risk agent (melatonin) if medication desired, and refer to sleep medicine for persistent/complex insomnia or suspected comorbid sleep disorder.
must_collect: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening pattern
current medications and OTC sleep aids
OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)
comorbidities affecting contraindications (hepatic disease, respiratory disease, substance use disorder)
steps: Elicit insomnia history and assess daytime impairment and duration to support diagnosis.
Screen for secondary causes, psychiatric comorbidity, substance use, and other sleep disorders (OSA/RLS/circadian).
Explain diagnosis using the term insomnia and provide brief education on contributing factors.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; include stimulus control and sleep restriction (or other CBT-I components).
State to avoid butobarbital/barbiturates and explain safety rationale.
Perform allergy, contraindication, and drug-interaction checks before prescribing any medication.
If medication is used, recommend a safer option (e.g., melatonin) with dosing and counseling; avoid combining with alcohol/sedatives.
Refer to sleep medicine for evaluation/CBT-I access and to assess for comorbid sleep disorders or refractory symptoms.

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates first-line therapy for iron deficiency anemia with a dosing strategy that balances efficacy and tolerability, enabling measurable clinical improvement.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact concepts 'iron deficiency anemia' and 'low iron' in patient-friendly language.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes 'iron supplement' and how to take it (dose/frequency, administration tips).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical) and evaluates contraindications to oral iron (critical) such as iron overload disorders, repeated transfusions/known hemochromatosis, or inability to take oral meds due to severe intolerance or malabsorption.

Addresses non-critical safety: reviews current medications/supplements to assess for drug interactions affecting absorption (e.g., antacids/PPIs, calcium, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones) and provides spacing instructions when relevant.
Includes counseling on expected side effects (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and mitigation strategies; warns about accidental ingestion risk in children and safe storage.

Plans evaluation for underlying cause of iron deficiency (especially occult blood loss) with appropriate history prompts (heavy menstrual bleeding, GI symptoms, diet, pregnancy status, recent blood donation) and recommends appropriate follow-up testing/assessment without requiring a specific READ-tool sequence.

Sets a follow-up timeframe and objective response criteria (e.g., symptom improvement and hemoglobin/reticulocyte response within ~2–4 weeks; recheck CBC/ferritin) and defines what to do if no response or intolerance occurs.

Avoids unsafe recommendations: does not prescribe iron if contraindications are present; does not ignore red flags for GI bleeding or severe anemia; does not provide dosing that exceeds typical oral elemental iron ranges without justification.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose iron deficiency anemia and start oral iron with safety checks, counseling, and follow-up to confirm response and address etiology.
must_collect: GI symptoms (melena, hematochezia, abdominal pain, reflux)
history of bariatric surgery or malabsorption
red flags (syncope, chest pain, dyspnea at rest)
steps: Explain that findings are consistent with iron deficiency anemia (low iron) and what that means.
Before prescribing, ask about medication allergies and assess contraindications to oral iron; review meds for interaction risks and provide spacing guidance.
Prescribe an oral iron supplement using an evidence-based dosing strategy (e.g., elemental iron 40–65 mg every other day or daily if tolerated).
Provide administration counseling (best absorbed on empty stomach; can take with food if GI upset; consider vitamin C; avoid taking with calcium/antacids; separate from interacting meds).
Counsel on adverse effects and safety (dark stools, constipation; hydration/fiber; keep out of reach of children).
Discuss likely sources of iron loss and need to evaluate the cause (menses vs GI loss vs diet/malabsorption) and advise appropriate next steps for workup.

Create a follow-up plan: reassess symptoms and repeat CBC (± reticulocyte count) in ~2–4 weeks; recheck ferritin/iron stores later; continue iron for ~3 months after normalization; escalate if no response or intolerance.

acceptable_variants.description: Daily low-dose oral iron rather than alternate-day dosing, with the same safety checks and follow-up response monitoring.
steps: Communicate diagnosis as iron deficiency anemia due to low iron.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks prior to prescribing; review medication list for interaction risks.
Prescribe elemental iron ~40–65 mg once daily (or equivalent formulation), adjusting for tolerance.
Provide absorption/spacing instructions and side-effect counseling.
Plan follow-up labs and duration of therapy; outline evaluation for bleeding or malabsorption if indicated.

acceptable_variants.description: Switch strategy if intolerance anticipated: start with alternate-day dosing and titrate, or choose a different oral formulation (e.g., ferrous gluconate) while maintaining core milestones and safety checks.
steps: Explain iron deficiency anemia (low iron) and treatment goals.
Check allergies, contraindications, and drug interactions before prescribing.
Prescribe an oral iron supplement with a tolerability-focused regimen (e.g., lower elemental iron dose or alternate-day dosing; different salt form).
Counsel on side effects, administration, and medication spacing.
Set follow-up and criteria for escalation if inadequate response.
